straight_beat.name: Terrence 'Terry' Boykin
straight_beat.column:
The Orioles lost again, but not because they’re bad. They’re not. They’re just… not good enough. Again. The Diamondbacks didn’t beat them. They outlasted them. The difference between a 4-3 loss and a 4-2 win is a single run, a single pitch, a single moment where the Orioles’ offense couldn’t capitalize on a chance. That’s the margin.  

Rutschman homered, Bradish pitched into the seventh, and Baltimore beat the Red Sox 4-2 at Camden Yards. That’s the script. That’s the story. But yesterday, the script didn’t play. The Orioles had 13 men on base. Thirteen. In a game where they needed to score two runs to tie. They didn’t. They couldn’t. Again.  

The bullpen’s been the story all year, and it’s not just the numbers. It’s the way they’ve clung to games, then unraveled in the late innings. Last night, they held a 3-2 lead into the eighth. A walk, a double, a wild pitch, and suddenly the D-backs were back in it. The margin between a win and a loss is razor-thin, but the Orioles keep finding ways to let it slip.  

This isn’t about the front office’s decisions. It’s about the box, the jar, the margin. The box of possibilities when you’re down a run. The jar of chances you have to make. The margin between a good team and a great one. The Orioles have the talent. They’ve had the talent since 202

optimist.name: Miz Dottie Hargrove
optimist.column:
The game was a tight one, a rollercoaster of pitches and pivotal plays, but let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. The Orioles lost yesterday, sure, but that’s just the universe testing our patience. You know what’s real? The young core of this team—Henderson, Holliday, Rutschman. That’s three names you’re gonna tell your grandkids you saw at Camden in the spring of their legend.  

Look, I get it. A 0.5-game gap in the AL East feels like a chasm right now. But let’s not forget, this is the same league that once saw the 1966 Orioles storm through the majors like a hurricane. Those kids back then didn’t just win games; they rewrote the story of baseball. And here we are, 56 years later, with a new generation of talent stepping into the spotlight.  

Henderson, the kid with the cannon arm and a smile that could light up Fells Point. Holliday, the heart of this team, swinging the bat like he’s got a destiny written in the stars. And Rutschman, the kid with the calm demeanor and a fastball that’s been clocked at 100 mph. These aren’t just players—they’re the future, hon. They’re the ones who’ll carry this team from the brink of contention to the heart of it.  

Sure, yesterday’s loss stings. But let’s not mistake a single game for a narrative. The Orioles are building something here, brick by brick, pitch by pitch. The front office is making moves that speak volumes—drafting wisely, trading for depth

pessimist.name: Roy Spanulli
pessimist.column:
The Orioles lost again. Not in a way that’s shocking, not in a way that’s unexpected, but in a way that feels like a ritual. Arizona’s 4-3 victory over Baltimore was another chapter in a story we’ve read before: a team with enough talent to win, but not enough focus to hold onto it. The scoreboard blinked, the fans groaned, and the front office blinked again, pretending they’re not the ones who’ve turned this into a circus.  

Peter Angelos lives on. Not in the way you’d hope. He’s not here, but his fingerprints are everywhere. The 100-win season? A memory now, buried under the weight of decisions that prioritized payroll over process. You think the Orioles are bad? Try explaining to the fans in Tampa how a team with a .500 record can still be the most exciting in the league. Or how a manager who’s been here longer than the current roster can’t keep players from looking over their shoulders.  

The AL East is a graveyard of hope. Toronto’s still a mess, but they’ve got a plan. Tampa’s got a plan. Even New York, for all its dysfunction, knows how to win. Baltimore? We’re just… here. The record is 9-8, but that’s not the point. The point is the way the team keeps folding under pressure. Like a crab shell in the heat of a summer afternoon—cracking, leaking, and somehow still clinging to the illusion of toughness.  

You can’t blame the players, not really. They’re trying. Some of
